title: C# Nullables
content:
### Nullable Types in C#

Nullable types allow value types (like `int`, `bool`, `double`) to represent `null` in addition to their normal range of values.

### Declaring Nullable Types

Add `?` after the value type:

```csharp
int? age = null;
bool? isComplete = null;
double? price = 9.99;
int?[] numbers = new int?[] { 1, null, 3 };
```

### Nullable<T> Struct

The `?` syntax is shorthand for `Nullable<T>`:

```csharp
Nullable<int> age = null;
int? age2 = null;  // Same as above
```

### Properties of Nullable Types

| Property | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `HasValue` | Returns true if value is not null |
| `Value` | Gets the actual value (throws if null) |

```csharp
int? number = null;
if (number.HasValue)
{
    Console.WriteLine(number.Value);
}
else
{
    Console.WriteLine("Number is null");
}
```

### Null Coalescing Operator (??)

Provides a default value when the nullable is null:

```csharp
int? maybe = null;
int definite = maybe ?? 0;  // definite = 0
```

### Null-Conditional Operator (?.)

Access members safely without null check:

```csharp
string? text = null;
int? length = text?.Length;  // null, not exception
```

### Null Coalescing Assignment (??=) (C# 8+)

Assigns a value only if the variable is null:

```csharp
int? count = null;
count ??= 10;  // count is now 10
count ??= 20;  // count stays 10
```

### Nullable Reference Types (C# 8+)

Warns at compile time about potential null references:

```csharp
#nullable enable
string name = null;  // Warning
string? nullableName = null;  // OK
```

> **Key Point:** Only value types can be made nullable with `?`. Reference types like `string` are already nullable by default. Use `??` to provide fallback values and `?.` for safe member access.

quiz.question: How do you declare a nullable int in C#?
